title: Chainsaw Warrior - blast and rip your way through the darkness (Review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yPh9jEw5_Lw

Daniel (@dungeondive) examines a 1987 Games Workshop relic that looks absolutely killer but plays like rolling dice until something happens to kill you. Chainsaw Warrior is maybe 99% luck, dressed up in the glossiest Judge Dredd fever dream ever printed. You roll stats, kit out your character with limited resources, and then march through a deck hoping darkness isn't in the last card and you have enough time to punch him.

The art is phenomenal—Brett Evans and Chris Baker doing 2000 AD–style goodness that makes you want to love this game. The rulebook is solid. The theme practically vibrates off the box. But underneath that polish? Character creation determines victory before you draw a single card. Roll badly for your equipment points? Congratulations, you've already lost. Missing the rope launcher means encountering an impassable chasm. Darkness positioned wrong in the deck? Tick-tick-tick goes your timer.

Daniel respects its place as a solo gaming ancestor from when such things barely existed. But he'd rather recommend *Escape the Dark Castle*, which delivers similar thrills with actual decisions sprinkled in. Chainsaw Warrior is fun for five minutes; it's expensive nostalgia after that.

----

How much luck is too much? Can a game still fascinate you if you know the outcome before you start?
